title: rgba_to_rgb allocates a full-image Vec on every encode call with alpha=false (the common RAW path)
description: Every call to encode_into (and encode_variant) on the no-alpha path calls rgba_to_rgb which Vec::with_capacity + extend_from_slice to produce a fresh N*3 byte buffer. For a 20 MP image that is a ~60 MB allocation per variant, repeated 3 times in the sequential path or once per rayon branch. The parallel path (feature=parallel) runs three encode_variant calls simultaneously, each doing its own rgba_to_rgb, so three 60 MB allocations are in flight at once. Since RAW images always have alpha=255 this is the hot path.

title: has_meaningful_alpha scans the full RGBA buffer (potentially 80+ MB) on every encode call
description: has_meaningful_alpha does a chunks_exact(4).any(|px| px[3] < 255) scan of the entire input buffer before any resizing or encoding. For a 20 MP RGBA image that is 80 MB of sequential reads just to determine a single boolean. The result is used by all three variant encodes but is computed from the full-resolution buffer even when a cheaper heuristic (e.g. sampling every N pixels, or trusting the caller-supplied SourceType::Raw flag) would work for the dominant RAW path.

title: perceptual_apply_bulk emits an eprintln timing call on every invocation, including production encode paths
description: The safe wrapper perceptual_apply_bulk (line 857-876, enabled by the c-perceptual feature) calls std::time::Instant::now() and eprintln!("perceptual_bulk_ms: {:.3}") on every call. This is inside the hot loop in process_into (TILE=64 pixels per call). For a 20 MP image this produces ~300,000 eprintln calls and an equivalent number of Instant::now() calls, both of which involve syscalls/mutex contention and will dominate the measured time. This is production code gated only by a cargo feature, not a test/debug gate.

title: PerceptualGrid::new() allocates and evaluates a 17^3 grid on the first pixel processed, inside the per-pixel apply_tone_math hot path
description: When perceptual_constancy=true and the c-perceptual feature is absent, the first call to apply_tone_math triggers PERCEPTUAL_GRID.with(borrow_mut) which calls PerceptualGrid::new(). This builds a 17*17*17*3=14739 f32 entry grid by running the full log-geodesic+Molchanov+hybrid path for each entry — 4913 transcendental calls (ln, exp, sqrt). This ~14 KB allocation and initialization happens inside the per-pixel hot path during the first rendered pixel, stalling it. Subsequent pixels use the cached grid but the initialization cost is paid without warning on the first call.

title: demosaic_rggb_planar_into fully demosaics to an interleaved buffer then deinterleaves it, doing twice the work vs a direct planar path
description: demosaic_rggb_planar_into calls demosaic_rggb (which writes interleaved RGB16 to a fresh Vec) and then deinterleaves that Vec into the caller-supplied planar buffers via a pointer loop. This means the full-image interleaved buffer is allocated and written, then completely read again for deinterleave — double the memory traffic of a direct planar write. The planar SIMD variant (demosaic_rggb_planar_simd on wasm) writes directly to planes and avoids this; the native planar path does not.

title: analyze() zero_padded allocates a full pixel buffer copy for any undersized input
description: When pixels.len() < px*4, analyze() calls zero_padded(pixels, px*4) which allocates a Vec<u8> of the full pixel size and copies into it, even when the buffer is only 1 byte short. In a streaming context where frames arrive in partial chunks this allocation is unavoidable given the current API, but the function could instead handle the tail in the scalar fallback without a full-buffer copy. This is a batch/time-lapse path so the impact depends on call frequency.

title: apply_perceptual_constancy recomputes scale from vib/sat from scratch on every call instead of accepting a precomputed scale parameter
description: apply_perceptual_constancy (the public Layer 5 API) computes the vibrance scale the same way as apply_tone_math's classic path, including the mx/mn/inv_mx saturation block for the vibrance-active case. This is a repeat of identical logic the caller could precompute once. Since this function is intended for per-pixel post-decode transform on progressive JXL frames, the duplicate computation adds overhead every call. The existing vib_zero_matrix / luma_weights helpers in tone_simd.rs show the precedent for precomputing such constants.

title: encode_rgba8_pyramid cascade copies the full resized thumbnail buffer instead of re-using it
description: In encode_rgba8_pyramid the cascade loop does `current = thumb` where thumb is a Vec<u8> from box_downscale_rgba8. This moves the buffer, but on the next iteration a new `thumb` is allocated via vec![0u8; ...] and then box_downscale_rgba8 writes into it from `current`. So each level allocates a new output buffer; there is no buffer reuse. For a 5-level pyramid this is 5 separate heap allocations of decreasing size. A simple two-buffer ping-pong would eliminate all intermediate allocations except the final encode input.

title: encode_rgba8_pyramid calls rgba.to_vec() to clone the full source buffer for the cascade initial current
description: At line 535 `let mut current = rgba.to_vec()` clones the entire source RGBA buffer (up to 80 MB for 20 MP) into a new heap allocation just to serve as the starting point for the cascade. The cascade immediately reads from it to downscale into a smaller buffer. Since encode_distance_into for the full-res level is called at the end with the original rgba reference (line 550), the clone of the full buffer is not needed for encoding — only for the first downscale step, which could read directly from rgba.

title: LUT_CACHE and PERCEPTUAL_GRID are thread_local but WASM and the parallel rayon path each have separate instances with no cross-thread sharing
description: The thread_local LUT_CACHE is rebuilt per thread when using rayon. The parallel path (lines 1217-1234) clones Arc<Vec> out of the cache (which is correct for sharing), but the initial ensure_lut call still builds three pre-LUTs and one post-LUT on first access per thread. For a batch encode of N images with the same params this pays the LUT build cost O(N_threads) times instead of once. An Arc-shared cache keyed on the params hash, populated on the first call and reused across threads, would reduce this to a single build.

title: bench_tone_split builds full 65536-entry pre-LUT instead of compact variant, making the benchmark measure a different code path than production
description: bench_tone_split calls build_pre_lut (65536 entries each for R, G, B) rather than build_pre_lut_compact or build_pre_lut_strided. Production process_into uses compact LUTs (build_pre_lut_compact). This means the benchmark measures cache behavior with a 128 KB/channel pre-LUT (likely L2/L3-resident) while production uses a 4096-entry or white-point-sized LUT that fits in L1. The benchmark results will not accurately predict production performance for the gather-bound LUT stage.
code_excerpt:
pub fn bench_tone_split(rgb16: &[u16], params: &PipelineParams) -> (f64, f64) {
    ...
    let pre_r = build_pre_lut(params.black, params.white, ti.wb_r, ti.exp_gain);  // 65536 entries
    let pre_g = build_pre_lut(params.black, params.white, ti.wb_g, ti.exp_gain);  // 65536 entries
    let pre_b = build_pre_lut(params.black, params.white, ti.wb_b, ti.exp_gain);  // 65536 entries
    ...

title: demosaic_rggb_planar allocates interleaved buffer then immediately discards it after deinterleave
description: demosaic_rggb_planar calls demosaic_rggb which allocates a Vec<u16> of width*height*3 elements (48 MB for 8 MP), writes the interleaved result, then the deinterleave loop reads all of it and writes to three separate output Vecs. The interleaved buffer is immediately dropped after deinterleave. Total peak memory is 4× the final output size. A direct planar demosaic (like the wasm simd path already implements) would eliminate the interleaved buffer entirely.

title: LUT build cost (3× pre + 1× post = ~4× 65536-entry fills) is not amortized for the first call in a batch; no lazy-init hook for pre-warming
description: ensure_lut on the first call to any process_* function builds four LUT tables synchronously. For compact LUTs this is 3×4096 + 65536 entries of f32 math including powf, mul_add, and clamp. In a batch encode with changing params per image, the LUT is rebuilt every time. There is no mechanism for callers to pre-warm the cache before the first pixel arrives. Adding a public warmup function (e.g. warm_lut_cache) would let server/batch callers amortize this outside a latency-sensitive context.
